speaker: interviewee
text: [TEST PATTERN TONE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Read that off for me. Say the date, who the interview is with, et cetera. All right. Go ahead. Me? Yes. December 5th, 1994. Survivor-- Victor Lewis. Interviewer-- Vera Brodsky. Flushing, New York, USA. The interview is in English. Whenever you're ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Could you tell us your name and where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Victor Lewis-- Leserkiewicz, full. I was born in Poland. 25th of August, 1919, in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- we were a family of five children and a grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Grandmother lived with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandmother lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- do you have-- could you give us the names of your brothers and sisters and when they were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Abraham. My mother's name was Berta. My oldest sister was Lola. Next one was Greta. Then I was [INAUDIBLE] in the middle. Then my brother is Leon. And my-- the youngest brother was Jacob.

speaker: interviewer
text: What occupation did your father have?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a business with textile business.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were Zionist, with middle-class family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What you mean by Zionist middle-class family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody believed in Zionism-- my father and the whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you at all planning to go to [INAUDIBLE] Palestine? No, I wouldn't say we all planned to go to the Palestine. But we all belonged to some kind of organization-- Zionist organization. My sister left for Palestine in 1932. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she came to visit in 1938-- or 1939-- sorry-- in 1930-- for Passover. And we thought that she's going to stay over longer, but she didn't like the tension in Europe, so she left for Palestine. And maybe a week or two weeks before, the war broke out. Yeah. Tell me, what sort of education did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had had junior high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Junior high school. A regular public school or a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regular public. No, a regular public school. I was living in a different-- in different-- in different--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a district? Like a borough?

speaker: interviewee
text: District. Not 100% Jewish. There was more Christians Jews in this district.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family belong to any-- were they affiliated with the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. My father-- my father was even a [NON-ENGLISH] in synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was Schornstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Schornstein. The name of the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was a man who built a synagogue. And his name was Schornstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was that synagogue in existence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, for a long time. For a long time. Even before I was born. It was a pretty long time.

words.text: It was a pretty long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did your family-- has your family-- did your family live in Poland altogether? How far can you trace your family back in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I can trace my family to my grandfather. But then I don't know, because my grandfather-- my grandparents died before-- before I was born-- from my father's side. From my mother's side, I remember my grandmother and grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me something about what it was like as a Jew living in Krakow before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jew living in Krakow depends where. And a Jew living in Krakow in the section where I used to live was different, because there were not too many Jews around. The school was-- was 85% to 90% Christian. And my class there were only three or four Jews. We have anti-Semitic and [? horroric ?] to [? say ?] like experiences between kids. And then when we were growing up, it was the same thing. Sometimes it came up to fistfight. But you had to be sure and to defend yourself. Otherwise, they could be always after you. So I can--

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you and your friends used to fight back?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I can say that I was fighting back. I was strong enough at my age to fight back. But the anti-Semitism was all over Krakow. My wife was living in this so-called Jewish ghetto. I was living on one side of the Vistula, and she was living on the other side of the Vistula, where only Jews were living. So it was different. In my-- in my section, when I was living there was more Polacks than Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you felt the-- the difference much more strongly.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why more Jews from my section belonged to Jewish organizations or Zionistic organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when you graduated from school?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I graduate from school--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately.

speaker: interviewer
text: '36. And you were how old in '36? About 18, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: 16, 17. What did I do? I went to work, and I wanted to become a druggist. Not a-- not a druggist like here, because we had two kind of-- we had apoteka. You remember it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had druggist. So, I want to be a druggist. And I was working for 2 and 1/2 years in that store. But before I got the license, my father got sick, and I had to jump into his business. And I was running his business on the age of 18 years, 17 or 18 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your older brothers? They were not in the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I didn't have older brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had the older sisters, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had older sisters, yeah. And when I started to work for my father, one of my sisters was already in Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, in 1938, you were running your father's business.

speaker: interviewer
text: '37, '38. And were you active in any Zionist organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I was active. The name was Akibah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Akibah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Akibah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Akibah. Very known in our hometown. We had a lot of children, and a lot of [INAUDIBLE] youth. And the groups were according to age, how old you were, you belonged to different [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was there for-- I belonged to this [INAUDIBLE] organization for approximately a year, year and a half. Then there was a created a new organization by the name of [NON-ENGLISH]. And I belonted to this organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any intentions-- and were there any preparations within the organizations for going on aliyah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did a lot of people go--

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides your sister--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, people were going to aliyah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister went on aliyah on not on her name. Palestine-- Jews from Palestine used the same passports to Poland. And on this passport, my sister went to Palestine on a different name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Polish government didn't permit Jews to leave, is that it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The English government didn't let the Jews into Palestine. So, if you had the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] that I don't know, like Palestine papers or passport, then they couldn't prove nothing that this-- that you are not from Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel, as the war approached between 1938, was there an increase in the anti-Semitism, the actions against Jews in Poland? Do you remember anything like that? Did you have any-- I mean-- did you read in the-- what about in the papers? What indications was there-- were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a Jewish newspaper by the name of [NON-ENGLISH]. It's not written in Jewish, but it-- it was owned by Jewish people. And all accidents and everything what was going on in the world was in this paper. For instance, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if some anti-Semitic things approached in Krakow, there were also boys and mens who were going back to-- to fight the anti-Semitism-- not only fight, but talk, and with-- with words, but also with fist. And it was-- there were clubs. There were a lot-- a lot-- a lot of beating. Myself, I had those feelings, also-- even  in school. Somebody told me 'dirty Jew' or something like that, so we fought. So we fought. We fought. That's why I was-- I was bloody, and the other guy was bloody. But Poland was very anti-Semitic country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it affect your father's business, the antisemitism? Was there any?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because my father was dealing with Jewish clients. And there were also some--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a retail business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Retail business. And also, Polish people, also. But those people know us very well. So they knew that-- like a-- like a friends. Some-- some Polish people were very good to Jews. But I would say 80% Polacks were antisemitic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Push you ahead a little bit to 1939. Were there any indications within the community that there was going to be a war? Did you have any warning, or anything at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1938, Nazis throw out Jews from Germany. Those Jews who didn't have-- who are not born in Germany, they throw them out, and they pushed them to Poland. Those Jews didn't have where to go. So I like I told you, that we belonged to an organization-- Zionist organization. We used to go out and place those people in homes, in Jewish homes. Matter of fact, I have a friend here [CLEARING THROAT] whom I placed to some people. And we are very good friends. People--

speaker: interviewer
text: By 1938, you had the Anschluss with Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anschluss, yeah, in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you had the Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewee
text: Krisallnacht. And that's a-- that's a time when Nazis throw out the Jews from-- from Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, what was-- yeah, what the reaction of the Polish community with what's going on next door practically?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish community wasn't so happy about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm talking about Jews. Jews were very helpful. Very helpful. They built kitchens for those Jews-- for those Jews-- to feed them. They tried to place them in homes. The [INAUDIBLE] matter of fact, in our house was a manly thing. We have five children at home, and three bedrooms-- one, two, three bedrooms. But we had the space, and one man was living between the boys. My uncle who lived in Vienna committed suicide with his whole family. I don't remember the date, but I heard it somewhere. After the Anschluss?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the Anschluss, right. He had two sons. And he opened a gas oven. Nobody knew about it. And that way was how he committed suicide, because of Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when your family finally found out about it, what was your reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was very sorry about it. And to prove this, when German came to Poland, I remember Gestapo came to our house, and they forced my father to sign some papers whom I later, after the war, knew that he signed a release from-- from the house where his brother owned.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. From Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's push forward a little bit. It's now 1939, the end of August.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. The war broke out. And I decided to go east. And I asked my father to go with us-- I mean my brother, my younger brother and me. We decided that we're going to go east. So, I asked my father to go with me. So he says, 'No, [INAUDIBLE], I cannot go.' He was a sick man. So, we two went. We made around 300 miles walking by-- walking. We came to a city named Tarnobrzeg.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's still in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still in Poland. Over the Vistula.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were getting closer to the Russian border, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were getting closer to the Russian border. But the Germans caught us out. So there was no way-- nowhere to go. So we decided we're gonna go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, we came back. It was Rosh Hashanah. This I can remember. Why? Because my father made a-- from [? other ?] room in our house, made a shul, because the shuls were closed. Then, when I came back, all shuls are closed, and people were playing in our house in one of our rooms.

speaker: interviewee
text: Further on, my-- my youngest brother was bar mitzvahed in 1940. So, I was staying outside on the street to watch him go. And the Gestapo, Germans or SS or somebody's not coming. Closer to our house. Shouldn't be heard that somebody's praying and lives in this apartment. That's what the-- the way how we were afraid of the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Nazis came into Krakow when? The war started--

speaker: interviewee
text: The war started September 2nd. And Nazis came to Krakow, I think-- because I was not at home then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You left-- you and your brother left right after the invasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Before the invasion, when we knew that they are already in [? villages ?] like Katowice. So we started to move out from home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, then, during this time, we-- I experienced when I came back, we experienced the a Pole who become a volksdeutscher, became a German. He came up to my father and said, 'Now, I'm going to move to your apartment.' And we got, I think, a day or two time to move out. So, I moved-- we moved out. Where did you move to?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father moved to outside the city, not in Krakow. They were in Niepolomice. I will tell you right away why this came out together. In the same time, we supposed to get Kennkarte, who is allowed to stay in the city of Krakow, and who has to leave the city of Krawkow. I and my younger brother, we got to Kennkartes. But the rest of my family didn't [INAUDIBLE], since they had to move anyhow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father took a apartment somewhere in a little village. The name of the village was Niepolomice. It was only 25 miles away from Krakow. And I was staying in Krakow with my brother, and they were living in Niepolomice. And we used to go to them to visit for Saturday-- Friday night to Saturday.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then ghetto come up. So we-- I mean, my brother and me moved into the ghetto. And my parents--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents moved into the ghetto, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In Niepolomice. In ghetto, like, you know you had to work. You had to go to work someplace. I became an auto mechanic. And I was working. Took a job by SS. One Jew between 20 Polacks. I was wearing armband on-- on a hand with the David star, and walking to work, and [INAUDIBLE], and living to work as long as they let you go alone. Later on, and months later, you couldn't go by yourself. You had to find a group. The SS people came, pick you-- picked a group up from ghetto, and brought to work, and brought them back to ghetto from work.

words.text: you know you had to work.
as long as they let you

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your father's store?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father [INAUDIBLE] in, like I told you, in Niepolomice for, I-- I would say, a year, till they start moving the other people who are back in small towns. They start to move them out, push them out from the-- from the town-- from the small town.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, we decided me-- my brother and me decided to bring them to ghetto of Krakow without the papers. And we smuggle-- smuggled the whole family in-- my mother, my father, my sister, and my youngest brother. My grandmother passed away a-- I think a year before, and take him to the ghetto. The ghetto was an enclosed area?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was enclosed area with barbed wire. And Gestapo, SS was watching the wires so nobody would-- could go out or in without permission. Sure, some people had the permit to leave the ghetto. You come back in-- in after work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was the life in ghetto. Ghetto was in one room there were living sometimes 10 and 15 people. And how did-- you maybe question me, how did you and where did you buy food? There was-- there were stores where Germans were allowed to bring some food into the ghetto. They couldn't starve everybody out. So there was some food in-- in stores in ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then you had to go and look in what store you could buy. You stay in line sometimes for a few hours until you get a piece of bread. And beside this, we were buying on black market. Where the piece of--

speaker: interviewer
text: The black market was from the Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Poles, right. From between the Pols and Jews. They made the money. Because if-- if bread was 50 cents, they charged $5 for it. And in ghetto, my experience was, at first the Gestapo gave the order that everybody had to have a Kennkarte. After the Kennkarte, maybe six months later, they came-- went over the Kennkartes and select people-- who is whom, who is doing work. And they put a blue sticker on the Kennkarte. This means they was allowed to stay in ghetto. And who couldn't stay in ghetto, they made a transport somewhere to concentration camps or other camps-- labor camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the-- can you describe what the in the ghetto was like? Did you think this was a temporary thing? Do you remember how you felt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody thought that this was gonna be a blitzkrieg, that today is the krieg, then tomorrow we're gonna be all right. And never in our mind was that the war going to last five years, like it was lasting. And people didn't believe from the beginning that the Germans, Nazis, were killing people. But I'm-- I'm already in ghetto, talking about the ghetto. Then we knew what-- what the life in ghetto was. German used to come inside, into the ghetto. If they didn't like somebody's face or something, took out a gun and killed them. If somebody had a beard, grow a beard, a Hasidic Jew or something, they cut him off, or they beat him up, or they kill him. That's what-- that was the life in-- in ghettos. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In 1942, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like I told you before--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. You went into the ghettos. The Germans formed the ghettos in 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I told you before that I brought my parents into ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1942 [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in October, there was the biggest deportation to gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't know it was deportation to gas chambers.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we didn't know where they was going to go. But we know the deportation was. I was witnessing, because I had a [GERMAN], which means 'papers' written that I'm working in a garage-- SS garage, and I was told that the-- the papers will help me to stay in ghetto. And there was an announcement to go home, and to--
